schools.summary: Chrysostom: Christ desired to break the sabbath, but not without cause — the disciples' hunger frees them from blame; admire their self-restraint, assailed by hunger yet not leaving Christ.
Augustine: charged with sabbath-breaking, not theft (Deut 23:25); Hilary and Rabanus read the corn-field figuratively — the field the world, the hunger the desire for men's salvation.
Luke's 'second-first sabbath' = the first after the second paschal day of the firstfruits sheaf; 'corn' = grain, not maize; the act lawful as to property but against the Pharisees' sabbath rules.
Henry: works of necessity are lawful on the sabbath; God provides for his people in the way of duty; Christ settles a point of use to his church in all ages.
Calvin: hypocrisy made them exact in trifles while sparing gross superstitions (Mt 23:23) — malevolence and envy more than superstition drove the censure.

The dispute is not the act (lawful, Deut 23:25) but its being done on the sabbath — plucking = reaping, rubbing = threshing, derivatives of the thirty-nine forbidden works; the watchers come down from Jerusalem (Lk 5:17).
Clarke: the Jews' sabbath superstition was 'inhumanity' (they would not defend themselves in war); an intolerant, censorious spirit in religion is a great curse.

David, fleeing Saul, ate the shewbread appropriated to the priests (1 Sam 21:6; Lev 24:9), the act passing uncondemned — proof that necessity does not bind; an argument a fortiori (Ellicott); David did 'what was not lawful,' the same charge (Robertson).

Calvin (first of five arguments): David was blameless, the Spirit commending the priest who gave the bread — ceremonies are not violated where godliness is not infringed; Jerome: man's preservation a sacrifice acceptable to God.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ as the true Vine buried in the garden, the curse of Adam rooted out.

schools.summary: 'The house of God' = the tabernacle at Nob, not the unbuilt temple; the shewbread twelve loaves offered each sabbath and reserved for the priests (Lev 24:5-9); the principle — when positive commands and the good of man collide, the latter prevails (Ellicott).

Clarke (with Parkhurst): the shewbread typified Christ, presented as sacrifice then becoming spiritual food to the spiritual priests (1 Pet 2:5); Wesley: the twelve loaves represented the twelve tribes.

The priests' sabbath labour — slaying victims, kindling fire (Num 28:9; against Ex 35:3) — would profane the day in any other, yet they are blameless; 'no sabbatism in the Temple' (Lightfoot); a stronger case than David's, done every sabbath by law.

Calvin: the duties of piety are not inconsistent — if temple-work sanctifies labour, much more the true spiritual service; 'profane' is accommodated to the hearers, proving only that temple-labours do not offend God.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the divorced wife and the returning husband (Deut 24), unrelated to the priests profaning the sabbath.

schools.summary: The better text is neuter — 'something greater than the temple'; Ellicott: the body of the Son of Man the truest temple, and man greater than any material temple (1 Cor 6:19); Barnes refers it to Christ's divine power over the laws, joining 'the sabbath was made for man' (Mk 2:27).

Henry: if the temple-service justified the priests, the service of Christ much more justifies the disciples; in Christ dwelt all the fulness of the Godhead bodily, so whatever is done in his name is accepted of God.

Hos 6:6 — moral, not positive, duties make up true religion; the teachers, having inverted the two, condemned the guiltless; a prophecy that escaped the sticklers for the letter (Robertson; cf. Mt 9:13); ignorance the cause of rash censure (Henry).

Calvin: rites are valued only as directed to their object, and inferior to deeds of kindness; hypocrites imitate piety by outward signs yet pervert it by the carnal worship alone; Clarke: four ways positive laws cease to oblige.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the history of persecution.

schools.summary: 'The Son of man' is Christ, not any man — the institutor of the sabbath, so its Lord (Gill); the title claims Messiahship yet acts as humanity's representative (Ellicott, Robertson); proof of his Godhead, sealed in the Lord's Day (Clarke; Rev 1:10).

Calvin: distinct from v.6 — here Christ declares authority to exempt his followers from the sabbath, relaxing it as other ceremonies; out of Christ the bondage of the Law is wretched, he alone giving the Spirit of adoption (Rom 8:15).
Wesley: they are guiltless as acting by his authority and attending him as the priests attended God; the Lord of the sabbath later changed it to the first day.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on Christ coming willingly to his Passion.

schools.summary: The Pharisees now accuse Christ himself; Jerome reports the Nazarene Gospel's bricklayer; Augustine harmonizes the Evangelists (another sabbath; the question and counter-question); Chrysostom: he set the man in the midst to subdue them; Hilary/Rabanus: the withered hand = the human race, healed by the hand stretched on the Cross.
He went into the synagogue (likely Capernaum's), Luke fixing it on another sabbath (Ellicott); the cure recorded for its sabbath setting — the work of mercy matching the disciples' work of necessity.

The Gloss: the Pharisees profane the sabbath by covetousness while charging Christ with profaning it by charity — we rest from evil deeds only (Lev 23:3); by nature our hands are withered, Christ alone curing (Henry).

themes: work of mercy on the sabbath

schools.summary: The right hand dried up, the man a hand-labourer; the question 'deniers' not inquirers, the rabbinic rules forbidding nearly all sabbath remedies (Lightfoot); they expected the cure and resolved to make it a ground of accusation, the permanent infirmity not a life-and-death case (Ellicott).
Calvin: they should have asked whether it was God's work or man's; God imposed on himself no restraint from sabbath operations — excessive folly to prescribe rules for God and restrain his freedom.

themes: a man with a withered hand

schools.summary: Grounded in the rabbinic canon 'take tender care of an Israelite's goods' — a beast fallen into a pit on the sabbath is lifted out (Lightfoot); self-interest 'a very decisive casuist' removing scruples in a moment (Clarke); Christ says it for our sakes (1 Cor 9:10).

Calvin: to withhold help is itself sin — little difference between manslaughter and neglecting one in distress; Christ charges them with trying, under a holy pretense, to compel him to sin by inaction.

themes: a sheep fallen into a pit

schools.summary: A man far better than a sheep, made in God's image and for whom Christ died (Barnes); 'by how much does a human being differ from a sheep?' — a question Christian civilization has not fully answered (Robertson); the conclusion recasts their question — it is lawful to do well.
Clarke: an argumentum ad hominem, confuting them on their own maxims; rebukes those who do more for a beast of pleasure than for a man for whom Christ died.

themes: how much better is a man than a sheep
works of mercy on the sabbath

schools.summary: A remarkable command the man might have pled inability to obey, yet being commanded it was his duty (Barnes); faith disregards apparent impossibilities where God commands and promises (Clarke); the cure by a word, no touch, left the sabbath unbroken and proved his Godhead.

Henry: by nature our hands are withered; Christ cures by putting life into the dead soul, working in us to will and to do; the command carries a promise of grace (Prov 1:23), leaving the lost inexcusable and the saved wholly indebted to grace.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on the name Beelzebul (belonging to v.24).

themes: emblem of grace healing the soul

schools.summary: A crisis in the hatred: the Pharisees conspire with the Herodians to kill Jesus (Mk 3:6); the council a deliberation of weight if the Jerusalem party shared it; envy and foiled argument the motive.
Henry: cursed malice — the real grievance his doctrine opposite to their pride and interest, the sabbath only a specious pretence; they took counsel not to imprison but to destroy.
Calvin: the obstinate rage of the wicked — the Law's chief teachers contriving murder like robbers; Christ's flight was not from fear but accommodation to our weakness, his hour not yet come.
Hilary/Jerome: he withdrew to remove from the Pharisees all opportunity of sin; Chrysostom (on the Isaiah prophecy): it was as easy for him to break them as a reed, yet he spared them.

themes: held a council to destroy him

schools.summary: He withdrew in Christian prudence, his hour not come, to the sea and into a boat (Mk 3:7); 'yield to the stream when you cannot stem it' (Clarke); he read their thoughts (Robertson); healed all that needed and sought it.

Henry: he declined not his work but his danger, choosing flight over miracle to submit to our sinless infirmities (Mt 10:23); his retirements filled with business — the people's Physician-general, doing good though the rulers persecuted him.

He would 'keep himself hid from those who would not acknowledge him' (Lightfoot, as with the parables); to avoid the plots and save his life (Barnes); Ellicott: not to hasten the final conflict, working while it was day, wise as serpents (Mt 10:16).
Calvin: not to suppress his fame wholly but to let it strike root and bear fruit at the proper season; he was manifested gradually (Gal 4:2), his glory shining even in concealment.
Henry: prudence, righteous judgment on the unworthy Pharisees, and humility — good men covet to do good, not to have it talked of, aiming at God's acceptance not men's applause.

themes: charged not to make him known
hid from those who would not know

schools.summary: The text is Isa 42:1-4, a free rendering; it corrects the expectation of a conquering Messiah — he was predicted peaceful, retiring, strengthening the feeble (Barnes); the prophecy owned Messianic by the Jews (Gill).

Calvin: Matthew shows the glory of Christ's divinity is not less admirable for appearing under a veil of infirmity; God appointed his Son a low appearance by decree, so to be offended at it is to be unworthy of salvation.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the Spirit's varied operations and Paul's confidence by the Spirit.

schools.summary: Christ God's 'servant' (Phil 2:7), 'chosen,' 'beloved' (Mt 3:17), in whom God is well pleased; Isaiah's mysterious 'servant of the Lord' finds its highest fulfilment in him; 'judgment' = the king's work of teaching righteousness, shown to the Gentiles (Ellicott).

Calvin: God points out by the finger the person he sends, that we not measure Christ by our sense; in his person the Father embraces the whole Church in love; the promised judgment is the kingdom spread through the world by word and Spirit.

Henry: our Saviour God's servant in redemption, chosen as equal to the undertaking, beloved from eternity, in whom God is well pleased — all our acceptance grounded on God's well-pleasedness in him (Eph 1:6; Jn 14:6).

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the one Spirit whose operations are various ('I will put my Spirit upon him').

schools.summary: He shall not strive in a wrangling way nor cry with reviling (Gill); meek and retiring, not seeking popularity (Barnes); a pervading calmness against the wrangling scribes, violent officers, and false revolt-leaders like Judas of Galilee (Ellicott).

Calvin: Christ's gentleness, voluntarily accommodating to our capacity, is gracious and necessary — were he to appear in glory it would swallow us up; yet mildness is not for all indiscriminately, the stubborn needing the hammer (anticipating v.20).
Henry: he carries on his undertaking without noise or ostentation — his kingdom spiritual, not advanced by force; the kingdom of God is not in word but in power.

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached (on Luke 21:18 / a hair of your head).

themes: he shall not strive nor cry

schools.summary: The bruised reed and smoking flax = the contrite and the weak believer, whom Christ cherishes rather than crushes (Barnes, Clarke); vivid images akin to Mt 11:28-30 (Robertson); the publican-apostle had tasted this tenderness himself (Ellicott).

Hilary: the bruised reed the Gentiles not broken but reserved for salvation, the smoking flax the spark not quenched in Israel; Jerome offers the reverse — the Jews the reed, the Gentiles the flax raised into a flame.

Calvin: Christ cherishes the weak (bruised reeds and smoking flax till kindled), but the prophet distinguishes weak from stubborn — the obstinate must have their hardness hammered; 'judgment unto victory' won only by fighting.
Henry: read of Christ's patience with the wicked, or his grace bearing up the weak — 'the day of small things is the day of precious things' (Zech 4:10), he dealing tenderly with true but weak grace.

debates.question: Whom do the bruised reed and smoking flax denote?

sides.position: the contrite / weak believer cherished by Christ
the Gentiles (reed) and the spark in Israel (flax)
the patiently-endured Jews (reed) and the Gentiles raised to flame (flax)

themes: a bruised reed shall he not break
smoking flax shall he not quench

schools.summary: Matthew follows the LXX ('trust in his name') against the Hebrew ('wait for his law'); the isles = the Gentiles, 'his name' = his gospel/law; 'shall hope,' Christ the sole hope; begun in the Idumeans, Tyrians, Sidonians attending him.

Henry: the great design of the gospel is to bring people to trust in Christ's name; fulfilling 'God shall persuade Japhet to dwell in the tents of Shem' (Gen 9:27); only they trust who wait for his law resolved to be ruled by it (1 Jn 3:23).

Three miracles in one — the blind sees, the dumb speaks, the possessed is freed (Jerome), daily fulfilled in conversion; the man a figure of the Gentile or unbeliever, demoniac, blind, and dumb until made meet to receive God (Hilary, Augustine).
Chrysostom: the demon had obstructed both inlets of faith, hearing and sight, which Christ opened.

Henry: a soul under Satan's power is blind in the things of God and dumb at the throne of grace; the dispossessing of Satan is the healing of souls — eyes opened to see God's glory, lips to speak his praise.

Henry: the question read as enquiring (cogent but transient impressions) or affirming (the kingdom now setting up); drawn by the common people, less prejudiced by worldly interest, so the way to the truth was plain (Isa 35:8).
'Son of David' the title of the promised Messiah; the cure had the same effect on the people as that of Mt 9:33; the wonder of the simple set deliberately against the rulers' blasphemy.

themes: Is not this the Son of David

schools.summary: Beelzebub from Baal — 'Zebub' a fly, so 'the man of flies,' the god of Ekron, the meanest name to cast on the Lord (Remigius); the multitude wondered, the Pharisees perverted what they could not deny (Rabanus).

Henry: irritated by the people's cry, they envied Christ as Saul did David (1 Sam 18:8); scornful ('this fellow') and blasphemous, forced to grant the miracle yet evading it by alleging a compact with the devil — the last refuge of obstinate infidelity.
'Beelzebul' the right form (JFB), the prince of the devils, ringleader of the apostasy; they ascribed the manifest miracle to Satan, unable to deny the fact.

themes: by Beelzebub the prince of devils
a compact with the devil alleged

schools.summary: Henry: Jesus knew their thoughts (which they kept in for shame), understanding our thoughts afar off; he answers the unspoken blasphemy with the self-evident maxim that a kingdom, city, or house divided against itself is brought to desolation.
An argument from common reason the Pharisees could not gainsay: internal division is ruin; to suppose Satan lending power to cast out his own is to arm his kingdom against itself.

The reductio: if Satan cast out Satan he is divided against himself, and his kingdom cannot stand; the casting out of devils proves not a league with Satan but the inbreaking of a stronger power, Satan being held by a fierce unity of malice.

Henry: Christ turns the slander back — by their own admission a kingdom is overthrown, and since Satan wars not against himself, the power must be divine, preparing for 'the kingdom of God is come unto you' (v.28).

'Your children' = the Jewish exorcists/Pharisees' disciples (cf. Acts 19:13); if casting out devils proves a league with Satan, their own children are condemned too — they must give up the argument or admit miracles prove God's aid.

Chrysostom: he says 'your children,' not 'my disciples,' leaving them occasion to return yet no impudent excuse; Jerome: judges 'not by authority but by comparison' — they ascribe such works to God but Christ's to the prince of devils.
Calvin: an unjust and malicious decision, condemning in Christ what they praised in their own — the objection 'of a personal nature,' aimed at the Pharisees' partiality, not the exorcists' merits.

themes: by whom do your children cast them out

schools.summary: The dilemma: the work superhuman, from Satan or God; Satan excluded, so by the Spirit (Luke's 'finger of God,' 11:20; Ex 8:19) — therefore Jesus is the Head of the divine kingdom; 'is come' = come unexpectedly upon you (Clarke).
Chrysostom: casting out devils is great power, not ordinary grace — 'if this be so, then is the Son of God come upon you'; why oppose your own salvation?
Calvin: Christ now treats them as convicted, charging ingratitude — God by revealing his Messiah was restoring his kingdom among them, while they drove him away.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the one Spirit whose operations are various.

themes: the kingdom of God is come

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Christ is at war with the devil, binding the prince and spoiling his goods — the contrary of their charge; Jerome: the house is the world, the strong man bound; Augustine: the 'goods' the unbelievers, freed from his power.
Ellicott: the strong man Satan, the house his realm, the goods the demons, the binding the check on his tyranny, the spoiling the final victory (Lk 11:22).
Calvin: Satan a strong tyrant who never yields of his own accord; deliverance is vain till he is subdued by a violent struggle — the scribes ignorant of the kingdom in resisting its beginnings.

outliers.note: Stray fragment marshalling the verse against dualist heretics who divide soul and body between two gods.

schools.summary: There are but two parties — to gather or scatter (Barnes, Robertson); Ellicott reconciles it with 'not against us is for us' (Lk 9:50) as opposite poles — no neutrality in the great struggle; the man casting out in Christ's name was with him, the slandering Pharisees against.
Jerome: it refers to the Devil, whose works (captivity, idols, sin) cannot agree with Christ's (freedom, knowledge of God, virtue); to think amiss of Christ is to be against him (Hilary).

Calvin: the scribes opposed in two respects — failing to assist and raging openly; the wider lesson that those who do not apply all means to advance the kingdom are unworthy of Christ's flock; Wesley: no neuters in this war.

themes: he that is not with me is against me

schools.summary: The sin defined by Mark's 'because they said, He hath an unclean spirit' (Mk 3:30) — ascribing Christ's Spirit-wrought miracles to the devil; all other blasphemy pardonable on repentance; Clarke comforts that no believer in Christ's mission can commit it.

Chrysostom/Jerome: blaspheming the Spirit by whom the works were wrought, against full knowledge; Augustine (at length): the blasphemy is final impenitence resisting the Spirit's gift of remission, of which we may despair of none while life lasts.
Remigius: forgiven only to the penitent (against Novatian); the Gloss: the verse destroys Origen's universalism; Gregory infers some lesser sins remitted in the world to come.

debates.question: What is the unpardonable blasphemy against the Holy Ghost?

sides.position: ascribing the Spirit's manifest works to the devil (the specific Beelzebub-charge)
final impenitence resisting the Spirit's gift of forgiveness

schools.summary: A word against the Son of man (reproaching his humble life as a Nazarene/Galilean) is pardonable; reproaching his divine character, attributing his power to Satan, is not; 'neither in this world nor the world to come' = 'hath never forgiveness' (Mk 3:29), not that sins are forgiven in hell.

Jerome: stumbling at his flesh, thinking him mere man, finds pardon for the body's lowliness, but calling the Spirit's works Beelzebub does not; Augustine: 'the word' (not 'any word') means the impenitence that resists the Spirit, since blasphemers find pardon on conversion.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the one Spirit's varied operations.

themes: word against the Son of man

schools.summary: 'The tree is known by his fruit' — words and works reveal the nature; Christ presses consistency: either own his works good and the tree good, or stand convicted of the absurdity of a good tree bearing evil fruit (the heart known by the conversation, Wesley).

Calvin: the likeness applied to speech, which detects the scribes' concealed malice; Christ waxes wroth against slander, for truth is highly valued; proverbs state the ordinary fact — the tongue is the index of the mind, bad speeches indications of a bad heart.

themes: make the tree good or corrupt

schools.summary: 'Generation of vipers' — venomous by nature; how can the evil speak good, since the mouth speaks from the heart's abundance? the word is the overflow of the inward store, betraying the heart at last.

Calvin: 'your heart is full of malice,' so no wonder you vomit wicked words; Christ thunders against hypocrites who pervert the right, an ordinary warning having no effect till they are sharply pierced; the Lord extorts confession by the tongue.

themes: out of the abundance of the heart
the heart the fountain of speech

schools.summary: The heart a treasure-store from which word and deed flow (Ellicott: 'a whole parable'); without the unity of goodness from the love of God, the treasure is predominantly evil; the good heart's treasure the love of God, the bad heart's the carnal mind (Clarke).

Gill: the 'good treasure' is grace implanted — not what a man is naturally but what is put into him, grace for grace out of Christ's fulness; the Pharisees' blasphemy issued from the evil treasure within.

themes: the good treasure of the heart
speech the outflow of the heart

schools.summary: The 'idle word' (argon) one that does nothing, vain and injurious (Clarke); Christ says not 'every evil word' but 'every idle word,' the random utterance that betrays character (Ellicott); judgment by words as Mt 25 is by deeds (Robertson) — an account given, not necessarily condemnation.
Chrysostom turns it to comfort the accused: sentence is by what you yourself spoke, so the accusers, not the accused, have cause to fear.

Calvin: an argument from the less to the greater — if idle words are questioned, how shall open blasphemy be spared? yet God who buries our deadly sins will pardon idle talk too — the threat a call to govern the tongue (Eph 5:4; Col 4:6).

themes: account in the day of judgment

schools.summary: Words the indication of the heart's principles, an important part of the ground of judgment (Barnes; Jas 3:6); Ellicott: 'justified' = the opposite of condemned — men justified by faith, by works, by words, all elements of character, words its most spontaneous manifestation.

Calvin: the Papists' use against justification by faith is childish — speech is not the ground of justification but pure speech absolves so we are not condemned by the tongue; the contrast of the two words confirms 'justify' = 'accounted righteous.'

outliers.note: Note here is mis-attached, on John's imprisonment (Mk 6:17-20).

themes: by thy words justified or condemned
words the evidence of the heart

schools.summary: Chrysostom: the irony — now railing, now fawning, calling him 'Master' to snare him; they sought 'a sign from heaven' (Jerome) — fire or thunder — though they could cavil even at those; he gives a sign 'from the deep beneath,' not heaven (Rabanus).

Henry: reasonable they should see a sign to prove his mission, but unreasonable to demand one when he had given so many; 'it is natural to proud men to prescribe to God, and then make that an excuse for not subscribing to him.'
Gill: they flatter him as 'Master,' willing to divert the discourse; all are not Christ's servants who call him Master (Henry); the demand full of hypocrisy after the miracle just wrought.

themes: a sign after so many signs

schools.summary: 'Adulterous' = unfaithful to the covenant figured as marriage (Isa 57:3; Hos 3:1), and literally so (Clarke); 'seeketh another sign' after so many; the sign of Jonah = the resurrection, evidence as the Ninevites had.

Calvin: the rebuke not for asking (Gideon, Hezekiah were not blamed) but for malice and ingratitude, despising his works and seeking a subterfuge; the one Jonah-likeness — he will be their prophet risen from the dead.
Henry: an adulterous brood, degenerate from their ancestors, and an adulterous wife departed from God; Wesley: such adulterers all who love the world.

'Three days and three nights' by the Jewish reckoning where a part of a day counts as the whole (Lightfoot's onah; Esth 4:16–5:1); the 'whale' a great fish/sea-monster, likely a shark; 'the heart of the earth' the grave, implying the descent into Hades; Ellicott defends the verse against the charge of a later insertion.
Henry: the grave to Christ as the fish's belly to Jonah, both prisoners — Jonah for his own sins, Christ for ours — both returning to the living.

outliers.note: Fragment drawing out the Jonah resemblance — sent to preach repentance, asleep in the storm, three days entombed.

schools.summary: 'Rise' = stand up as witnesses, condemning 'by the comparison of their example' (Jerome, Ellicott); heathen repented at a mere man's word, the Jews not at the Son of God's; Christ greater than Jonah in person, audience, calls, miracles.
Remigius: one resurrection of good and bad, judgment following hard upon it (against the heretics' two); Jerome/Rabanus: the faith of the Gentiles set above Israel's.
Calvin: ungodly men repented at an unknown foreigner, while the sanctuary of doctrine hears not the Son of God — so we are worse than all unbelievers if Christ now speaking from heaven move us not.

The queen of Sheba (1 Kgs 10:1), of Arabia or Ethiopia; 'the uttermost parts of the earth' = a great distance; she sought Solomon herself, exceeding even Nineveh's case (Chrysostom); the chapter's marvellous claim — greater than the Temple, Jonas, Solomon (Ellicott).

Henry: in many particulars her zeal condemns us — she had no invitation, came over difficulties, sought but worldly wisdom, could only hear Solomon; we are invited, have Christ among us, may have wisdom to salvation, Christ made of God our Wisdom.

themes: came from afar to hear wisdom

schools.summary: Applied to the Jewish people: the demon of idolatry cast out at the Law, the relapse worse in refusing Christ (Chrysostom); Jerome insists the close compels reference to the Jews, not heretics; the 'dry places' the hearts of the Gentiles or the righteous (Remigius, Gregory).

Christ speaks 'according to the ancient opinions' that evil spirits abode in desolate regions (Rev 18:2; Isa 13:21), the demon finding no rest out of men's bosoms; a parable of relapse after a brief reformation.
Calvin: the going out magnifies grace — the devil cast out whenever Christ shines on us; but vanquished, he whets his rage, so a sharper contest follows our calling (1 Pet 5:8).

The man the demon's 'house' — empty of good, swept of gross vice by outward reform, garnished with 'the showy trifles of folly' (Clarke); a two-fold symbolism of the man left to conventional morality and of the nation (Ellicott); the demon's irony of leaving 'for a walk' (Robertson).

Calvin: believers indwelt by the Spirit are fortified, but Satan's highest delight is the emptiness produced when man, parting with Christ, sets no value on grace; Wesley: empty of God, swept of the Spirit's fruits, garnished with levity and security.

outliers.note: Stray fragment on the new birth of water and the Spirit.

themes: I will return into my house

schools.summary: Jerome: the Jews now possessed by more demons in blaspheming Christ than in Egypt, the sevenfold number set against the seven spirits of grace (Isa 11:2); Augustine: the 'seven other' a sevenfold hypocrisy filling the cleansed but uninhabited soul.

Henry: sinners awakened, if they grieve away the Spirit, become worse than before — seven devils dwell where one did; the brief stir of conviction, issuing in no true cleansing, leaves the nation more deeply possessed, the heavier judgment near.

The closing words 'even so unto this wicked generation' fix the application on the nation's relapse; a universal lesson — a soul reformed but not inhabited by Christ is an open door to a sevenfold return of evil.

themes: the last state worse than the first

schools.summary: The kinsmen came thinking him 'beside himself' (Mk 3:21) — 'to seize upon him' as distracted (Clarke); the throng so great he was not stopping to eat (Mk 3:20; Robertson); the 'brethren' near kinsmen, standing without.

Henry: the passage shows Christ's love for his followers, that much as he loved his mother he loved his disciples more; the interruption gives occasion for the great saying on spiritual kinship; Calvin: Mary acted improperly in interrupting his discourse.

themes: his mother and brethren stood without

schools.summary: Robertson: the verse omitted by the leading MSS (Aleph, B, L, Old Syriac), genuine in Mk 3:32 and Lk 8:20, probably copied into Matthew; Gill harmonizes the reports that 'more than one' told him, as first one then another apprised him while he delayed to go out.

No want of affection — his life proved his love for his mother (Jn 19:26); the question asked 'to fix the attention' on who held the nearest relation, the tie to his disciples more tender than even that to his mother; they came thinking him distracted (Clarke; Mk 3:21).

Calvin: the words reprove Mary's eagerness in interrupting his teaching, while teaching that Christ admits all disciples to the rank of his nearest relatives — no tie more sacred than spiritual relationship (2 Cor 5:16); Wesley: knowing why they came justifies his seeming disregard.

The disciples — not the twelve only but all present who truly believed (Gill); a dramatic wave of the hand (Robertson); spiritual relationships above natural, the latter yielding when they clash (Ellicott; Mt 10:37); his real family all who follow him.

Ellicott: the words a strong Protestant protest, all the stronger for its absence of polemic, against the cultus of the Virgin — excluding that her intercession is mightier than another saintly soul's; Wesley: the highest severity to his natural, goodness to his spiritual relations.

themes: behold my mother and my brethren

schools.summary: 'Doing the will of the Father' = the obedience of faith, to believe in Christ (Gill; Jn 6:40), not perfect law-keeping; the ground of spiritual relationship, not the mere fact of baptism but what it signifies (Barnes); the 'sister' named suggests she too had joined the interruption (Ellicott).

Calvin: not that men perfectly fulfil the law, but commending faith, the source of obedience that covers the flesh's defects; Christ, honouring lawful natural duties, yet sets spiritual relationship far above, bestowing the honour of being his brethren — base ingratitude not to pursue it.

themes: whosoever shall do the will of my Father
the same is my brother and sister and mother
